content:
Hey all!

I recently came across this particular issue. I guess it's complaining about the following pattern:

#ifdef CONFIG_LOCKDEP
typedef struct lockdep_map *lockdep_map_p;
#define mt_lock_is_held(mt)                                             \
    (!(mt)->ma_external_lock || lock_is_held((mt)->ma_external_lock))

#define mt_write_lock_is_held(mt)                   \
    (!(mt)->ma_external_lock ||                 \
     lock_is_held_type((mt)->ma_external_lock, 0))

#define mt_set_external_lock(mt, lock)                  \
    (mt)->ma_external_lock = &(lock)->dep_map

#define mt_on_stack(mt)         (mt).ma_external_lock = NULL
#else
typedef struct { /* nothing */ } lockdep_map_p;  <-------------------

I wonder if this can be safely ignored? Or maybe the solution is to somehow tweak the bindgen invocation?

Hey everyone, this seems like a legit issue.

Any struct embedding a lockdep_map_p will eventually generate this warning if CONFIG_LOCKDEP is unset.

@Andreas Hindborg

I see this is the case for struct gendisk, i.e.:

pub type blk_mode_t = core::ffi::c_uint;
#[repr(C)]
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]
pub struct gendisk {
    pub major: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub first_minor: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub minors: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub disk_name: [core::ffi::c_char; 32usize],
    pub events: core::ffi::c_ushort,
    pub event_flags: core::ffi::c_ushort,
    pub part_tbl: xarray,
    pub part0: *mut block_device,
    pub fops: *const block_device_operations,
    pub queue: *mut request_queue,
    pub private_data: *mut core::ffi::c_void,
    pub bio_split: bio_set,
    pub flags: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub state: core::ffi::c_ulong,
    pub open_mutex: mutex,
    pub open_partitions: core::ffi::c_uint,
    pub bdi: *mut backing_dev_info,
    pub queue_kobj: kobject,
    pub slave_dir: *mut kobject,
    pub slave_bdevs: list_head,
    pub random: *mut timer_rand_state,
    pub sync_io: atomic_t,
    pub ev: *mut disk_events,
    pub cdi: *mut cdrom_device_info,
    pub node_id: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub bb: *mut badblocks,
    pub lockdep_map: lockdep_map,  <----------
    pub diskseq: u64_,
    pub open_mode: blk_mode_t,
    pub ia_ranges: *mut blk_independent_access_ranges,
}

Where

/*
 * The lockdep_map takes no space if lockdep is disabled:
 */
struct lockdep_map { };

Did you notice that?
I did not. We recently had an issue that only appeared when lockdep is enabled. I usually build without lockdep.
Do you have a reproducer I can try?
Is it during bindings generation?

@Andreas Hindborg will get back with a repro

@Danilo Krummrich @Lyude any similar issues with struct drm_file?
I always run lockdep enabled and didn't notice anything so far.
I don't understand the issue from the first post you gave, what is the error that you get?

@Andreas Hindborg but no, the bindings are generated just fine, since Rust supports ZSTs. The warning is emitted is when you try to use a binding with a ZST in an extern "C" function.

@Danilo Krummrich this only happens with lockdep disabled, since that's when they use this trick:

typedef struct { /* nothing */ } lockdep_map_p;

ZSTs are not officially supported in C, so I guess this achieved through a compiler extension, which means Rust is right to emit a warning here.

@Benno Lossin Yeah I should have worded this better, the error is in the title:

`extern` block uses type `kernel::bindings::lockdep_map_p`
ahh I see, I was searching for error :)
so the kernel C compiler supports ZSTs? if that is the case, we might ask the Rust folks for a flag to disable the warning?
I'm just wondering why I don't get the warning
I never pass those structs by value, just by pointer.
Maybe that is it?

So normally lockdep defines lockdep_map as ZST if LOCKDEP=n, and only uses pointers of it. From Daniel's case, it's the maple tree who defines a "pointer" to lockdep_map as ZST. That's the difference here.
unfortunately, if you #[allow(improper_ctypes) , this can actually hide other (valid) warnings. As Benno said, maybe we should ask for a more granular way to deal with this?
Could you share the config and code?
I don't there is a problem for the existing code, so I'm curious about your case that triggers this.

@Boqun Feng

https://gitlab.collabora.com/dwlsalmeida/for-upstream/-/commits/improper_ctypes
config
Hmm.. I hit a few different errors from the branch you shared.
Could you share a full compilation log when you hit the error?

Ok, now I can reproduce this with CONFIG_DRM=y and CONFIG_DRM_PANTHOR_RS=y.
do you guys have the same rust version?

Benno Lossin said:

do you guys have the same rust version?

It should be a config mis-match issue. Now I can reproduce.
@Boqun Feng CONFIG_DRM and CONFIG_DRM_PANTHOR_RS are both y in the config I sent.

So if I manually change this

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct lock_class_key { }

into

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct lock_class_key;

I don't see the issue again. Is it because these two definitions are different, or it's the tool that fails to detect this?
there shouldn't be a difference between the two
What we are hitting is Rust will treat any struct containing a ZST like above not FFI-safe, which I don't understand why

Boqun Feng said:

So if I manually change this

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct lock_class_key { }

into

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct lock_class_key;

I don't see the issue again. Is it because these two definitions are different, or it's the tool that fails to detect this?

One second, maybe the change doesn't do anything, it's just the warning will only be reported once.

Boqun Feng said:

Boqun Feng said:

So if I manually change this

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct lock_class_key { }

into

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
struct lock_class_key;

I don't see the issue again. Is it because these two definitions are different, or it's the tool that fails to detect this?

One second, maybe the change doesn't do anything, it's just the warning will only be reported once.

Oh yes, this seems to be the case, so cancel that observation.
So why a struct containing a #[repr(C)] ZST is not FFI-safe then? Because C standard doesn't have ZST? @Gary Guo
Minimal repro: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=1b097e141cd380e9159de73511d5c52a

Boqun Feng said:

So why a struct containing a #[repr(C)] ZST is not FFI-safe then? Because C standard doesn't have ZST? Gary Guo

Empty structs have size of 1 in C++.
In C it's undefined behaviour and it's a GNU extension that makes it zero size. MSVC will just reject it.
but currently Rust will treat this as size 0, right?
Yes
If you give it a field with size 0 then Rust shouldn't complain anymore.
https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=4abe5dfab36131dfdc7ac72e7c6f32e3
For reference https://godbolt.org/z/nz967WrGv
I think all we need to do is to ask bindgen to treat this type as opaque

Gary Guo said:

I think all we need to do is to ask bindgen to treat this type as opaque

@Daniel Almeida does this sound like a plan to you? Do you have any other concern?

Trevor Gross said:

For reference https://godbolt.org/z/nz967WrGv

For reference of the C++ behaviour: https://godbolt.org/z/f77Gf6GYe
(just out of curiosity) how could we define ZST in C++?
Add char dummy[0];
I think this is GNU extension again
What fun rules - you can't just say you want nothing. You have to say you want something, but in a quantity of nothing :smile:
@Boqun Feng to treat what type as opaque, exactly? The ZST, or the struct that embeds the ZST?
The ZST
I am fine with that. Can we tweak the bindgen invocation to do so only if CONFIG_LOCKDEP (for example) is not set? If it is set, the problem simply does not exist
I don't think there's an issue to always treat lockdep_map_p as opaque?
yeah, I see no issue as well tbh...
As long as we don't have FFI functions that take lockdep_map_p as value

Danilo Krummrich said:

I always run lockdep enabled and didn't notice anything so far.

Same - I usually try to keep it on (especially since it's been useful for figuring out the spinlock bindings)
